name: Wavelength: Sleep & Jet Lag
subtitle: Time your light, sleep better

screenshots.caption: Know if the light around you right now is helping or hurting tonight's sleep
screenshots.alt: Daytime reading: 350 melanopic lux, at target, this light is shifting your sleep earlier

screenshots.caption: It tells you exactly when to get bright light and when to stay out of it
screenshots.alt: Outdoor reading: 300 melanopic lux, this light is strengthening your circadian rhythm

screenshots.caption: After sundown the whole app goes dark and confirms your room is dim enough to sleep
screenshots.alt: Night mode: 3 melanopic lux, well under limit, good, keep it dim

screenshots.caption: Resetting to a new time zone or schedule? It walks you through every step
screenshots.alt: Reading: 350 melanopic lux, this light is shifting your sleep later, avoid caffeine now

description:
The light you sit in all evening is quietly setting the time your body wants to sleep, and you cannot see it. Wavelength can. Point your iPhone camera at the room and it reads the light reaching your eyes, then tells you in plain words whether it is pulling tonight's sleep earlier or pushing it later.

That is the part most people are missing. If you struggle to fall asleep at the hour you want, the bright lamps and screens around you in the evening are a likely reason. Wavelength makes that light visible and tells you what to do about it.

Most circadian apps stop halfway. A light meter hands you a number and walks away. A coaching app builds you a sleep plan but never sees the light you are actually in. Wavelength does both. It measures your real light and coaches your real schedule.

SEE YOUR LIGHT

Point your camera at the light around you. Wavelength gives you an instant color-coded reading and a clear verdict: at target, well under the limit, or too bright for this hour. No setup, no wearable, no accessories.

GET TOLD WHAT TO DO

A reading on its own changes nothing. Wavelength weighs your current bedtime, the bedtime you are aiming for, the time of day, and the direction you want to move, then tells you to get light or avoid light right now and when the next window opens.

DAILY REMINDERS

Set your current bedtime and your goal bedtime, and Wavelength schedules the nudges that matter:
- When to start dimming before bed
- When bright light will pull your sleep in the right direction
- When light would work against you

NIGHT MODE

After sundown the whole app fades to near-black so the screen itself stays out of your way, and it confirms your room is dim enough to sleep.

JET LAG AND NEW SCHEDULES

Landing in a new time zone, or moving to an earlier or later schedule, runs the same model. Wavelength tells you when to chase the sun and when to hide from it, so you wake on local time instead of fighting your body for a week.

DESIGNED FOR

- People who want to fall asleep at the hour they actually want
- Travelers who want to beat jet lag and wake on local time
- Shift workers holding a rhythm through nights: protect daytime sleep, stay alert on shift
- Anyone trying to move a clock that has drifted too late

BUILT ON PUBLISHED SCIENCE

Wavelength estimates melanopic light, the blue-rich light that sets your body clock, and judges it against the expert-consensus thresholds in Brown et al. (2022), PLOS Biology:
- Daytime: bright light for alertness
- Evening: low light before bed
- Sleep: near darkness

The timing model, how light at a given hour shifts your clock, follows Hannay et al. (2019), Journal of Biological Rhythms.

Research links better-aligned sleep to steadier mood and daytime focus. Wavelength supports sleep and circadian alignment. It does not diagnose, treat, or cure any medical condition.

WHAT YOU NEED

An iPhone with a camera. Nothing else. Camera measurement is approximate, not a laboratory spectrometer, but it is well-suited to the moment-to-moment comparisons that matter for your body clock.
note:
This is a preview, not the live App Store listing.
Screenshots are the exact 6.7" PNGs from the app build, shown bare as Apple displays them. The third frame is the app's night mode: the whole UI fades to near-black before bed.
